🤔
Why Do You Want to Join the Civil Services?
🇬🇧 English
Ask yourself one simple question: why do you want to join the Civil Services?
If your answer is to serve the nation, uphold the Constitution, improve people's lives, promote justice, and contribute to good governance — you are in the right place.
But if your primary motivation is wealth, power or status, pause and reflect. This is a solemn responsibility, not just a profession.
Serve with integrity. Lead with compassion. Build the nation.
🇮🇳 हिंदी
अपने आप से एक सरल प्रश्न पूछिए — आप सिविल सेवा में क्यों आना चाहते हैं?
यदि आपका उत्तर राष्ट्र की सेवा, संविधान के मूल्यों की रक्षा, जनकल्याण और सुशासन है — तो आपका हार्दिक स्वागत है।
किन्तु यदि उद्देश्य केवल धन, पद या प्रतिष्ठा है, तो एक क्षण रुककर पुनर्विचार कीजिए। यह केवल नौकरी नहीं, उत्तरदायित्व है।
सेवा को धर्म बनाइए, सत्यनिष्ठा को मार्ग और राष्ट्रहित को सर्वोच्च उद्देश्य।

BharatCivilServices
Know Your Institutions

About UPSC & SSC

Before you prepare for these exams, it helps to know where they came from — their origin, evolution, and what they actually do today.

What is UPSC?

The Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) is India's central recruiting agency, established as a constitutional body under Articles 315–323 of the Constitution of India. It conducts the Civil Services Examination (CSE) and several other exams to recruit officers for the IAS, IPS, IFS, and 20+ other Group A & B central services. UPSC is headquartered at Dholpur House, New Delhi, and functions independently of executive interference, with its own constitutionally protected budget and tenure-secured members.

1854 — Macaulay Committee Report
Recommended that recruitment to the Indian Civil Service be made through open competitive examinations rather than patronage — the philosophical seed of today's UPSC exam.
1858 — Crown Takes Over
After the 1857 revolt, administration passed from the East India Company to the British Crown; civil service recruitment was formalised, but exams were held only in London, excluding most Indians.
1886 — Aitchison Commission
Recommended holding a statutory civil service examination in India itself, splitting services into Imperial, Provincial, and Subordinate tiers.
1919 — Government of India Act
First proposed setting up a Public Service Commission in India to handle recruitment, following recommendations of the Islington Commission (1917).
1926 — First Public Service Commission
Established on 1 October 1926 under Sir Ross Barker, following the Lee Commission's recommendations — the direct institutional ancestor of UPSC.
1935 — Federal Public Service Commission
The Government of India Act 1935 renamed and expanded it to the Federal Public Service Commission, with provincial Public Service Commissions also established.
1950 — UPSC Born (Constitutional Status)
On 26 January 1950, with the commencement of the Constitution, the Federal PSC became the Union Public Service Commission — a permanent constitutional body, independent of the executive.
1979 — Kothari Committee Reforms
Recommended the modern three-stage pattern — Preliminary Exam, Main Exam, and Personality Test (Interview) — that defines the CSE structure to this day.
2011 — CSAT Introduced
Civil Services Aptitude Test (Paper II of Prelims) was introduced to test reasoning and comprehension, alongside the traditional GS paper.
2013 — Single Optional Paper
Following the Nigavekar Committee's recommendations, the Mains optional subject was reduced from two papers to one, with greater weightage shifted to GS and Essay.

What is SSC?

The Staff Selection Commission (SSC) is a central government organisation under the Department of Personnel & Training, Ministry of Personnel, Public Grievances and Pensions. Unlike UPSC, SSC is not a constitutional body — it is an executive body created through a government resolution. It recruits staff for Group B (non-gazetted & some gazetted) and Group C posts across central government ministries, departments, and subordinate offices through exams like CGL, CHSL, MTS, JE, CPO, and Stenographer.

1975 — Commission Established
Set up on 4 November 1975 as the "Subordinate Services Commission," tasked with recruiting staff for posts that didn't fall under UPSC's purview.
1977 — Renamed "Staff Selection Commission"
The Commission was renamed to its present name, broadening its scope beyond purely "subordinate" posts to include a wider range of Group B & C roles.
1990s — Expansion of Examinations
SSC began conducting distinct standardised exams for different post categories — clerical, inspector-level, and technical posts — replacing fragmented departmental recruitment.
1999 — SSC CGL Introduced
The Combined Graduate Level Examination (CGL) was introduced as SSC's flagship exam for graduate-level Group B & C posts like Inspector, ASO, and Auditor.
2010s — Regional Network Expansion
SSC strengthened its structure of one Headquarters (New Delhi) plus 9 Regional/Sub-Regional offices to manage exams and grievances across India.
2016 — Shift to Computer-Based Tests
SSC CGL Tier-I moved fully to Computer-Based Test (CBT) mode, improving transparency, faster results, and reducing paper-leak risks compared to OMR-based exams.
Present — Widening Mandate
Today SSC conducts CGL, CHSL, MTS, JE, CPO, Stenographer, GD Constable (with other agencies), and Selection Post exams — making it the largest-volume recruiter among India's central exam bodies.

Sources: Constitution of India, official UPSC & SSC websites, and Ministry of Personnel records. Always verify current rules from official notifications.

BharatCivilServices
Know Your Country, Inside Out

About India

From the smallest national symbol to the biggest macro number — everything a civil services aspirant must know about India, organised exam-wise.

India At a Glance

India — officially the Republic of India (Bhārat Gaṇarājya) — is a Union of States with a federal parliamentary system, a Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic per the Preamble. Capital: New Delhi. Independence: 15 August 1947; became a Republic on 26 January 1950. National motto: "Satyameva Jayate" (Truth Alone Triumphs — from the Mundaka Upanishad).

🚩 National Flag
Tiranga — saffron (courage), white (truth/peace) with Ashoka Chakra (24 spokes, dharma), green (prosperity). Adopted 22 July 1947. Khadi/silk only, per Flag Code of India.
🦁 National Emblem
Lion Capital of Ashoka (Sarnath, 3rd century BCE) — four lions, adopted as State Emblem on 26 January 1950. Inscription "Satyameva Jayate" in Devanagari beneath.
🎵 National Anthem
"Jana Gana Mana" — written by Rabindranath Tagore (Bengali), adopted 24 January 1950. Singing time: 52 seconds.
🎶 National Song
"Vande Mataram" — by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay (from Anandamath, 1882). Equal status with the National Anthem.
🐅 National Animal
Royal Bengal Tiger — symbol of strength; protected under Project Tiger (1973).
🦚 National Bird
Indian Peacock (Pavo cristatus) — protected under the Wildlife (Protection) Act, 1972.
🪷 National Flower
Lotus (Nelumbo nucifera) — symbol of purity and divine beauty.
🌳 National Tree
Banyan Tree (Ficus benghalensis) — symbol of longevity and shelter.
🥭 National Fruit
Mango (Mangifera indica) — "King of Fruits".
🌊 National River
Ganga — declared National River in November 2008.
🐬 National Aquatic Animal
Gangetic River Dolphin (Platanista gangetica) — also state animal of Assam & UP.
🐘 National Heritage Animal
Indian Elephant — declared in 2010.
₹ Currency
Indian Rupee (INR); the ₹ symbol was officially adopted in 2010, designed by D. Udaya Kumar.
📅 National Calendar
Saka Calendar — adopted 22 March 1957, used alongside the Gregorian calendar for official/government purposes.
🗣️ Official Languages
Hindi (Devanagari script) is the official language of the Union; English is the associate official language. 22 languages are listed in the Eighth Schedule.
📐 Quick Facts
Area: ~3.287 million km² (7th largest country). Population: ~1.43 billion (world's most populous, 2023 UN estimate). States & UTs: 28 States + 8 Union Territories.

Physical Setting

India lies entirely in the Northern Hemisphere, between latitudes 8°4'N and 37°6'N and longitudes 68°7'E and 97°25'E, with a coastline of approximately 7,516.6 km and a land frontier of about 15,200 km shared with seven countries.

🧭 Neighbours
Land borders: Pakistan, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Afghanistan (via PoK). Maritime neighbours: Sri Lanka and Maldives.
⛰️ Physical Divisions
The Himalayas, Northern Plains (Indo-Gangetic), Peninsular Plateau, Coastal Plains (Eastern & Western), Thar Desert, and the Island groups.
🏔️ Highest Point
Kangchenjunga (8,586 m), on the India–Nepal border in Sikkim — the highest peak entirely/partly within India.
🌊 Major Rivers
Himalayan: Ganga, Brahmaputra, Indus (and tributaries — Yamuna, Chenab, Sutlej). Peninsular: Godavari (largest peninsular river), Krishna, Kaveri, Narmada, Tapi, Mahanadi.
🏝️ Island Groups
Andaman & Nicobar Islands (Bay of Bengal, volcanic/tectonic origin) and Lakshadweep (Arabian Sea, coral origin).
🌤️ Climate
Tropical monsoon type; four seasons — Winter (Dec–Feb), Summer (Mar–May), South-West Monsoon (Jun–Sep), Retreating/Post-Monsoon (Oct–Nov).
🟤 Soil Types
Alluvial (largest area, Indo-Gangetic plains), Black/Regur (Deccan, cotton-growing), Red & Yellow, Laterite, Arid/Desert, Mountain/Forest soils.
🌲 Forest Cover
~21.7% of India's geographical area under forest cover (India State of Forest Report); biodiversity hotspots include the Western Ghats, the Himalaya, Indo-Burma, and Sundaland (Nicobar Islands).
🕐 Time Zone
A single time zone — Indian Standard Time (IST), UTC+5:30 — based on the 82.5°E longitude passing through Mirzapur, UP.
🗺️ Largest & Smallest States
Largest by area: Rajasthan. Smallest by area: Goa. Largest by population: Uttar Pradesh. Smallest by population: Sikkim.

From Civilisation to Republic

India's recorded history spans over 4,500 years — from the urban Indus Valley Civilisation to the world's largest constitutional democracy today. UPSC Prelims & Mains test Ancient, Medieval, and Modern History extensively — this timeline covers the civil-services-relevant skeleton.

~2500–1500 BCE — Indus Valley Civilisation
Bronze Age urban civilisation (Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, Dholavira) — planned cities, drainage systems, undeciphered script.
~1500–600 BCE — Vedic Period
Composition of the four Vedas; transition from pastoral Early Vedic to agrarian Later Vedic society; rise of Janapadas.
6th century BCE — Rise of Buddhism & Jainism
Gautama Buddha and Mahavira founded their respective traditions, challenging Vedic ritualism.
321–185 BCE — Maurya Empire
Chandragupta Maurya founded the first pan-Indian empire; Ashoka's Dhamma and edicts mark a major moment in Ancient India.
320–550 CE — Gupta Empire
Called India's "Golden Age" — advances in mathematics (zero), astronomy, Sanskrit literature (Kalidasa), and art.
1206–1526 — Delhi Sultanate
Five dynasties — Slave, Khilji, Tughlaq, Sayyid, Lodi — ruled North India, introducing Indo-Islamic architecture and administration.
1526–1857 — Mughal Empire
Babur to Bahadur Shah Zafar; peak under Akbar, Jahangir, Shah Jahan (Taj Mahal), Aurangzeb; centralised administration, Mansabdari system.
1757 — Battle of Plassey
British East India Company defeats Siraj-ud-Daulah, marking the start of formal colonial control over Bengal.
1857 — The Great Revolt
First large-scale uprising against British rule; led to the Crown taking direct control via the Government of India Act, 1858.
1885 — Indian National Congress Founded
A.O. Hume helped found the INC; W.C. Bonnerjee was its first President — the principal vehicle of the freedom movement.
1915–1947 — Gandhian Era
Mahatma Gandhi's return from South Africa; Non-Cooperation (1920), Civil Disobedience/Dandi March (1930), Quit India Movement (1942).
15 August 1947 — Independence & Partition
India gained independence; simultaneous Partition created Pakistan, causing one of history's largest mass migrations.
26 November 1949 / 26 January 1950 — Constitution
Constitution adopted 26 Nov 1949, came into force 26 Jan 1950 — India became a sovereign democratic Republic; Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee.

The World's Longest Written Constitution

India is governed by the longest written constitution in the world — originally 395 Articles, 22 Parts, and 8 Schedules; today, after amendments, it has grown to 470+ Articles, 25 Parts, and 12 Schedules. It establishes a federal structure with a strong unitary bias, a parliamentary system of government, and an independent judiciary with the power of judicial review.

🏛️ Three Organs of State
Legislature (Parliament — Lok Sabha 543 seats + Rajya Sabha 245 seats), Executive (President, Vice-President, PM & Council of Ministers), Judiciary (Supreme Court + 25 High Courts + subordinate courts).
👤 President of India
Constitutional/nominal Head of State, elected by an Electoral College (elected MPs + elected MLAs) under Article 54; 5-year term.
👤 Prime Minister
Real executive head of government, leader of the majority party/coalition in Lok Sabha, appointed by the President under Article 75.
⚖️ Supreme Court
Apex court & guardian of the Constitution; original, appellate, and advisory jurisdiction; power of judicial review under Article 32 (writ jurisdiction).
🗺️ Federal Structure
28 States + 8 Union Territories; powers divided via the Seventh Schedule into Union List, State List, and Concurrent List.
🏘️ Local Self-Government
73rd Amendment (1992) — three-tier Panchayati Raj in rural areas; 74th Amendment (1992) — Municipalities in urban areas.
🗳️ Election Commission of India
Constitutional body (Article 324) conducting elections to Parliament, State Legislatures, and the offices of President & Vice-President; headed by the Chief Election Commissioner.
📜 Fundamental Rights & DPSP
Part III (Articles 12–35) guarantees Fundamental Rights (justiciable); Part IV (Articles 36–51) lays down Directive Principles of State Policy (non-justiciable, guide governance).
📚 Schedules & Parts
12 Schedules cover everything from state/UT lists (1st) to anti-defection law (10th) to Panchayats/Municipalities (11th/12th).
🔄 Amendment Procedure
Article 368 — amendments can be by simple majority, special majority, or special majority + ratification by half the states, depending on the provision.

A Fast-Growing, Diversifying Economy

India is among the world's largest economies by nominal GDP and the third-largest by purchasing power parity (PPP), and is consistently one of the fastest-growing major economies. GS Paper III (Mains) draws heavily on economic concepts, recent Budget themes, and the Economic Survey.

💵 Currency & Central Bank
Currency: Indian Rupee (₹). Central Bank: Reserve Bank of India (RBI), established 1 April 1935, nationalised in 1949 — regulates monetary policy, banking, and currency issuance.
📊 Sectoral Composition
Services sector contributes the largest share of GDP (~50–55%), followed by Industry (~25–30%) and Agriculture (~15–18%) — though agriculture still employs the largest workforce share.
🌾 Agriculture
Major crops: rice, wheat, sugarcane, cotton, pulses, oilseeds. India is a leading global producer of milk, pulses, and spices.
📈 Stock Exchanges
Bombay Stock Exchange (BSE, est. 1875) — Asia's oldest stock exchange; National Stock Exchange (NSE, est. 1992) — India's largest by trading volume.
🧾 Tax Reform — GST
Goods & Services Tax launched 1 July 2017 — "One Nation, One Tax," subsuming most indirect central & state taxes (VAT, Excise, Service Tax).
🏗️ Planning History
Five-Year Plans ran from 1951 to 2017 (Planning Commission); replaced by NITI Aayog (National Institution for Transforming India) in January 2015, a policy think-tank model.
🌐 External Sector
Major exports: petroleum products, gems & jewellery, pharmaceuticals, engineering goods, IT & IT-enabled services; India is a top global exporter of generic medicines and software services.
💳 Financial Inclusion
Pradhan Mantri Jan Dhan Yojana (2014), UPI (Unified Payments Interface, 2016) — India now runs one of the world's largest real-time digital payments systems.

The World's Most Populous & Most Diverse Nation

India overtook China in 2023 to become the world's most populous country, per UN estimates. It is also among the most linguistically, religiously, and culturally diverse nations on Earth — a recurring theme across GS Paper I (Society) and Essay papers.

👥 Population
~1.43 billion (2023 UN estimate) — the world's most populous country; median age around 28–29 years, giving India a significant "demographic dividend."
⚖️ Sex Ratio
943 females per 1,000 males (2011 Census) — a key indicator tracked alongside schemes like Beti Bachao Beti Padhao.
📚 Literacy Rate
~74% overall per 2011 Census (rising since); significant gender and rural-urban literacy gaps remain a policy focus area.
🏘️ Urban-Rural Split
~69% rural, ~31% urban per 2011 Census — though rapid urbanisation continues to shift this balance.
🛐 Religious Composition
Per 2011 Census: Hindu ~79.8%, Muslim ~14.2%, Christian ~2.3%, Sikh ~1.7%, Buddhist ~0.7%, Jain ~0.4%, others/unstated the remainder. India is constitutionally secular.
📋 SC/ST Population
Scheduled Castes ~16.6% and Scheduled Tribes ~8.6% of the population (2011 Census) — protected under Articles 341 & 342 and various reservation policies.
🗣️ Linguistic Diversity
22 languages in the Eighth Schedule; the Census records over 19,500 mother tongues, grouped into 121 languages — among the most linguistically diverse nations in the world.
🏘️ Panchayati Raj Coverage
Over 250,000 Gram Panchayats and 2.5+ lakh elected local-body representatives — the world's largest grassroots democratic network.

A Civilisation of Continuous Living Heritage

India's cultural depth — spanning classical arts, architecture, festivals, and Nobel-recognised achievements — is regularly tested in GS Paper I (Art & Culture) and forms a recurring theme in Essay and Interview rounds.

🏛️ UNESCO World Heritage Sites
India has 40+ UNESCO World Heritage Sites — including the Taj Mahal, Red Fort, Qutub Minar, Ajanta & Ellora Caves, Khajuraho, Hampi, Sun Temple Konark, and natural sites like Kaziranga & the Western Ghats.
💃 Classical Dance Forms
Eight classical dance forms recognised by the Sangeet Natak Akademi: Bharatanatyam, Kathak, Kathakali, Odissi, Manipuri, Kuchipudi, Mohiniyattam, and Sattriya.
🎉 Major Festivals
Diwali, Holi, Eid-ul-Fitr, Christmas, Pongal/Makar Sankranti, Onam, Durga Puja, Baisakhi, Navratri, Gurpurab — reflecting India's multi-religious calendar.
🏗️ Architectural Styles
Dravidian (South Indian temples), Nagara (North Indian temples), Indo-Islamic (Mughal — Taj Mahal, Red Fort), and Indo-Saracenic (colonial-era hybrid, e.g. Victoria Memorial).
📖 Ancient Literature
The four Vedas, Upanishads, and the two great epics — the Ramayana and the Mahabharata (containing the Bhagavad Gita) — form the bedrock of classical Indian thought.
🏆 Nobel Laureates of Indian Origin
Rabindranath Tagore (Literature, 1913), C.V. Raman (Physics, 1930), Mother Teresa (Peace, 1979), Amartya Sen (Economics, 1998), Kailash Satyarthi (Peace, 2014), Abhijit Banerjee (Economics, 2019).
🎬 Cinema
India produces more feature films annually than any other country in the world, spanning Hindi (Bollywood) and major regional industries — Tamil, Telugu, Malayalam, Bengali, Marathi, and more.

Strategic, Scientific & Sporting Milestones

India's space, defence, and sporting achievements feature prominently in Current Affairs and GS Paper III (Science & Tech / Security) — and are a favourite source of Interview questions.

🚀 ISRO & Space Programme
Indian Space Research Organisation, established 1969. Mangalyaan (2014) made India the first Asian nation to reach Mars orbit, and the first in the world to do so on its maiden attempt. Chandrayaan-3 (2023) made India the first country to land near the Moon's south pole.
☢️ Nuclear Programme
Department of Atomic Energy oversees civil & strategic nuclear assets; key tests — Pokhran-I "Smiling Buddha" (1974) and Pokhran-II (1998), after which India declared itself a nuclear weapons state.
🪖 Armed Forces
Indian Army, Navy, and Air Force — among the largest standing militaries in the world by active personnel; supported by DRDO (Defence Research & Development Organisation) for indigenous defence technology.
🐅 Conservation
Project Tiger (1973) helped India become home to over 70% of the world's wild tiger population, spread across 50+ designated Tiger Reserves.
🏏 Cricket World Cups
India has won the ICC Cricket World Cup (ODI) in 1983 and 2011, and the ICC T20 World Cup in 2007 and 2024.
♟️ Chess
Viswanathan Anand — five-time World Chess Champion; D. Gukesh became the youngest-ever undisputed World Chess Champion in 2024.
🥇 Olympics
India has historically excelled in field hockey (multiple Olympic golds) and has steadily expanded its medal tally in shooting, wrestling, badminton, javelin, and athletics in recent Games.
🌍 Global Standing
World's largest democracy by population; a founding member of the UN, NAM (Non-Aligned Movement), G20, and BRICS; permanent member aspirant of the UN Security Council.

Note: Population, GDP, literacy, and similar dynamic figures are approximate and based on the latest available Census/official estimates — always cross-check exact current figures against Census of India, RBI, NITI Aayog, and Economic Survey publications before quoting them in exams.

BharatCivilServices
India's Place in a Connected World

About The World

World geography, history, organisations, and economy — the global backdrop every GS Paper I, II & III answer needs to be set against.

The World, In Numbers

Planet Earth is divided into 195 countries (193 UN member states + 2 observer states — the Holy See and Palestine), spread across 7 continents and 5 oceans, home to a global population of roughly 8.1 billion people (2024 estimate). UPSC's GS & IFS syllabi regularly draw on this global canvas — physical geography, world organisations, and international current affairs all matter.

🌍 Continents (by Area)
Asia ~4.46 crore km² (largest), Africa ~3.04 crore km², North America ~2.45 crore km², South America ~1.78 crore km², Antarctica ~1.42 crore km², Europe ~1.02 crore km², Australia/Oceania ~85 lakh km² (smallest).
🌊 Oceans (by Area)
Pacific ~16.5 crore km² (largest & deepest), Atlantic ~8.5 crore km², Indian ~7 crore km², Southern (Antarctic) ~2 crore km², Arctic ~1.4 crore km² (smallest & shallowest).
🏳️ Countries
195 sovereign states recognised by the UN framework — 193 full UN member states plus 2 UN observer states (Holy See/Vatican and Palestine).
👥 World Population
~8.1 billion (2024 estimate). India and China together account for roughly a third of all humanity; Asia alone hosts about 60% of the world's population.
🗣️ Most Spoken Languages
By total speakers: English, Mandarin Chinese, Hindi, Spanish, French, and Arabic are consistently among the most widely spoken languages worldwide.
🏔️ Largest & Smallest Countries
Largest by area: Russia. Smallest by area: Vatican City. Most populous: India (since 2023, overtaking China). Least populous (sovereign): Vatican City.
🧭 Prime Meridian & Equator
The Prime Meridian (0° longitude) passes through Greenwich, London, and defines GMT. The Equator (0° latitude) passes through 13 countries, including Brazil, Kenya, and Indonesia.
🕐 International Date Line
Roughly follows 180° longitude through the Pacific Ocean — crossing it eastward subtracts a day, crossing westward adds one.

Physical & Political Geography

World physical geography — landforms, climate systems, and natural extremes — is a core component of GS Paper I, often tested alongside India-specific geography for comparative questions.

⛰️ Highest Mountain
Mount Everest (8,849 m), on the Nepal–China (Tibet) border — the highest point above sea level on Earth.
🌊 Deepest Point
Challenger Deep, in the Mariana Trench (Pacific Ocean) — roughly 10,935 m below sea level, the deepest known point in any ocean.
🏜️ Largest Desert
Antarctica is technically the largest desert (cold desert) by area; the Sahara is the largest hot desert, spanning North Africa.
🌊 Longest River
The Nile (Africa) and the Amazon (South America) are generally cited as the two longest rivers in the world, with measurement debates between the two.
🌲 Largest Rainforest
The Amazon Rainforest, spanning Brazil and neighbouring countries — often called the "lungs of the Earth" for its role in global oxygen and carbon cycles.
🏞️ Largest Lake
The Caspian Sea (technically a saltwater lake, bordered by 5 countries) is the largest inland body of water; Lake Superior is the largest freshwater lake by surface area.
🌡️ Climate Zones
Broadly classified (Köppen system) into Tropical, Dry/Arid, Temperate, Continental, and Polar climate zones, based on temperature and precipitation patterns.
🌋 Ring of Fire
A horseshoe-shaped zone around the Pacific Ocean basin associated with ~90% of the world's earthquakes and most active volcanoes.
🗺️ Tallest Waterfall
Angel Falls, Venezuela — the world's tallest uninterrupted waterfall at ~979 m.

Key Turning Points in World History

World History is a defined sub-topic in UPSC Mains GS Paper I — covering events from the Industrial Revolution to the World Wars and decolonisation, which shaped the world order India operates within today.

~3100 BCE — Ancient Egypt Unified
Upper and Lower Egypt united under the first Pharaoh, beginning one of history's earliest and longest-lasting civilisations.
~500 BCE — Classical Greece
Birth of democracy in Athens, and foundational developments in philosophy (Socrates, Plato, Aristotle), science, and theatre.
27 BCE–476 CE — Roman Empire
One of history's largest empires, shaping law, engineering, governance, and language (Latin) across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.
1760s–1840s — Industrial Revolution
Began in Britain — transition to machine-based manufacturing, steam power, and factory systems — fundamentally reshaped global economies and colonial demand for raw materials.
1789 — French Revolution
Overthrew the monarchy, popularised ideals of "Liberty, Equality, Fraternity" — hugely influential on modern democratic and revolutionary movements worldwide.
1914–1918 — World War I
Triggered by the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand; ended empires (Austro-Hungarian, Ottoman, Russian), redrew European borders via the Treaty of Versailles (1919).
1917 — Russian Revolution
Bolshevik Revolution led by Lenin overthrew the Tsarist regime, eventually establishing the Soviet Union (USSR) — the world's first major communist state.
1939–1945 — World War II
The deadliest conflict in human history; ended with Allied victory, the founding of the United Nations (1945), and the beginning of the Cold War era.
1945–1991 — Cold War
Geopolitical and ideological rivalry between the USA (capitalist bloc) and USSR (communist bloc); ended with the dissolution of the Soviet Union in 1991.
1947–1991 — Decolonisation Wave
Mass wave of independence movements across Asia and Africa, beginning with India's independence in 1947, reshaping the global political map.
1989 — Fall of the Berlin Wall
Symbolised the end of the Cold War divide between East and West, leading to German reunification in 1990.

The Architecture of Global Governance

International organisations and India's role within them form a major chunk of GS Paper II (International Relations) — one of the highest-yield topics for both Prelims and Mains.

🇺🇳 United Nations (UN)
Founded 1945, headquartered in New York; 193 member states. Key organs: General Assembly, Security Council (5 permanent members with veto — USA, UK, France, Russia, China), ICJ, ECOSOC, Secretariat.
💰 IMF & World Bank
Both born from the 1944 Bretton Woods Conference. IMF focuses on monetary cooperation & financial stability; the World Bank focuses on development financing and poverty reduction.
🌐 World Trade Organisation (WTO)
Established 1995 (successor to GATT, 1947), headquartered in Geneva — sets and enforces rules for international trade between member nations.
🤝 G20
Group of 20 major economies (19 countries + EU + African Union), representing ~85% of global GDP; India held the G20 Presidency in 2023.
🌏 BRICS
Originally Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa — an emerging-economies grouping promoting multipolar global governance and South-South cooperation, later expanded to include additional members.
🕊️ NAM (Non-Aligned Movement)
Founded 1961, with India (Nehru), Yugoslavia (Tito), and Egypt (Nasser) as key founders — countries choosing not to formally align with either Cold War bloc.
🌏 SAARC & SCO
SAARC — South Asian regional cooperation body (8 members). SCO (Shanghai Cooperation Organisation) — Eurasian political, economic & security grouping that India joined as a full member in 2017.
🏥 WHO
World Health Organisation, headquartered in Geneva, est. 1948 — the UN's specialised agency for international public health, coordinating responses to global health emergencies.
🌡️ UNFCCC & COP
UN Framework Convention on Climate Change drives annual COP summits — the Paris Agreement (COP21, 2015) is the landmark global climate accord India is a signatory to.
⚖️ ICJ & ICC
International Court of Justice (The Hague) — settles disputes between states under the UN Charter. International Criminal Court (also The Hague) — prosecutes individuals for genocide, war crimes, and crimes against humanity (India is not a signatory to the Rome Statute).

The Global Economic Order

Comparative world economic indicators help frame GS Paper III answers around India's relative economic standing, trade relationships, and global financial architecture.

💵 Reserve Currencies
The US Dollar remains the world's dominant reserve currency, followed by the Euro, Japanese Yen, British Pound, and a growing share for the Chinese Yuan.
📊 Largest Economies (Nominal GDP)
The United States and China consistently rank as the two largest economies by nominal GDP, with India among the top five — rankings shift year to year with growth and exchange rates.
🏭 Global Trade Hubs
Major global financial & trade centres include New York, London, Singapore, Hong Kong, Shanghai, and Dubai — each playing distinct roles in global finance and logistics.
🛢️ OPEC
Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries — a cartel of major oil-producing nations coordinating output and pricing; significantly influences global energy markets and India's import bill.
🌐 Free Trade Areas
Examples include the European Union's Single Market, ASEAN Free Trade Area, and USMCA (USA-Mexico-Canada) — reducing trade barriers among member states.
📉 Global Inequality
The Gini coefficient and Human Development Index (HDI, published by UNDP) are key metrics used to compare income inequality and overall development across nations.

A Demographically Shifting Planet

Global demographic trends — ageing populations, urbanisation, and migration — increasingly shape international relations and are tested across GS Paper I & II.

👥 Most Populous Countries
India and China together hold the top two spots, followed by the United States, Indonesia, and Pakistan among the most populous nations.
🏙️ Urbanisation
More than half of the world's population now lives in urban areas, with this share projected to keep rising — particularly across Asia and Africa.
👴 Ageing Societies
Japan, South Korea, and much of Western Europe face rapidly ageing populations, in contrast to India's relatively young demographic profile.
🌐 Migration
The UN tracks well over 250 million international migrants worldwide — driven by labour opportunities, conflict, and climate displacement.
🗣️ Global Linguistic Diversity
Roughly 7,000 living languages are spoken worldwide today, though a small fraction account for the vast majority of speakers.
📚 Human Development Index (HDI)
Published annually by the UNDP, HDI combines life expectancy, education, and per-capita income into a single comparative score for ranking countries' development levels.

Shared Human Heritage

World heritage and cultural landmarks frequently appear in Prelims as quick factual recall questions, and add depth to Essay and Interview answers.

🏛️ New 7 Wonders of the World
Great Wall of China, Petra (Jordan), Christ the Redeemer (Brazil), Machu Picchu (Peru), Chichen Itza (Mexico), Colosseum (Italy), and the Taj Mahal (India) — selected via a global poll in 2007.
🗿 Ancient Wonder Survivor
Of the original Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, only the Great Pyramid of Giza (Egypt) still survives largely intact today.
🏛️ UNESCO World Heritage
Over 1,200 sites worldwide are inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List, spanning cultural, natural, and mixed heritage categories across nearly every country.
🎨 Major World Religions
Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, and Judaism rank among the world's major religious traditions by number of adherents and global spread.
🏟️ Global Sporting Events
The Olympic Games (Summer & Winter, every 4 years) and the FIFA World Cup are the world's most-watched recurring sporting events.
🎬 Global Award Recognitions
The Nobel Prizes (Physics, Chemistry, Medicine, Literature, Peace, Economics) remain the most prestigious international honours across science, literature, and global diplomacy.

Frontiers, Flashpoints & Global Watch-list

Fast-evolving global science and geopolitics are best tracked through the Current Affairs and News Digest sections of this platform — this tab gives the durable, exam-relevant backdrop.

🚀 Global Space Race
NASA (USA), ESA (Europe), Roscosmos (Russia), CNSA (China), and ISRO (India) are the major state space agencies; private players like SpaceX have transformed commercial spaceflight.
🌡️ Climate Commitments
The Paris Agreement (2015) commits signatory nations to limiting global warming to well below 2°C; India has pledged to reach net-zero emissions by 2070.
🤖 AI & Tech Governance
Global debates over AI regulation, data privacy, and semiconductor supply chains have become central to 21st-century geopolitics and trade policy.
⚔️ Active Global Flashpoints
Ongoing regional conflicts and territorial disputes (e.g., Russia–Ukraine, Middle East tensions, South China Sea) continue to shape global diplomacy — track these via the Current Affairs section for the latest developments.
🦠 Global Health Security
The COVID-19 pandemic (declared by WHO in 2020) reshaped global health cooperation, vaccine diplomacy, and supply-chain resilience planning worldwide.
🇮🇳 India's Global Diplomacy
India pursues "multi-alignment" — engaging simultaneously with the US, Russia, the EU, and regional groupings like the Quad (with the US, Japan & Australia), reflecting a strategically autonomous foreign policy.

Note: Geopolitical alignments, rankings, and global statistics change frequently — always cross-check the latest figures against the UN, World Bank, IMF, and the Current Affairs section before quoting them in exams.

BharatCivilServices
India's Administrative Map

Indian Districts, States & Union Territories

Every state, every Union Territory, district counts, formation history, and the special constitutional provisions that govern them.

28 States of India

Each state listed with its capital and the year it attained its present form (most via the States Reorganisation Act, 1956, or subsequent bifurcations). Capitals/years occasionally change administratively — always cross-check with the latest State Gazette before exams.

#StateCapitalArea (km²)Present Form Since
1Andhra PradeshAmaravati162,9681956 (bifurcated 2014)
2Arunachal PradeshItanagar83,7431987
3AssamDispur78,4381950
4BiharPatna94,1631950 (Jharkhand carved out 2000)
5ChhattisgarhRaipur135,1922000 (from Madhya Pradesh)
6GoaPanaji3,7021987 (former UT)
7GujaratGandhinagar196,2441960 (from Bombay State)
8HaryanaChandigarh44,2121966 (from Punjab)
9Himachal PradeshShimla55,6731971 (full statehood)
10JharkhandRanchi79,7162000 (from Bihar)
11KarnatakaBengaluru191,7911956 (renamed from Mysore, 1973)
12KeralaThiruvananthapuram38,8521956
13Madhya PradeshBhopal308,2521956
14MaharashtraMumbai307,7131960 (from Bombay State)
15ManipurImphal22,3271972 (full statehood)
16MeghalayaShillong22,4291972 (carved from Assam)
17MizoramAizawl21,0811987
18NagalandKohima16,5791963 (first state carved from Assam)
19OdishaBhubaneswar155,7071936 (pre-independence)
20PunjabChandigarh50,3621966 (post-reorganisation)
21RajasthanJaipur342,2391956 (final integration)
22SikkimGangtok7,0961975 (merged with India, 36th amendment)
23Tamil NaduChennai130,0601956 (renamed from Madras, 1969)
24TelanganaHyderabad112,0772014 (29th & newest state)
25TripuraAgartala10,4861972 (full statehood)
26Uttar PradeshLucknow240,9281950
27UttarakhandDehradun (interim)53,4832000 (from Uttar Pradesh)
28West BengalKolkata88,7521950

8 Union Territories

Union Territories are administered directly by the Union Government, typically through an Administrator/Lieutenant Governor appointed by the President (Article 239). Delhi, Puducherry, and Jammu & Kashmir have partial legislatures of their own.

#Union TerritoryCapitalArea (km²)Key Note
1Andaman & Nicobar IslandsPort Blair8,249Bay of Bengal island territory; administered by a Lieutenant Governor.
2ChandigarhChandigarh114Joint capital of Punjab & Haryana, but a UT itself since 1966.
3Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & DiuDaman603Merged into a single UT in January 2020.
4Delhi (NCT)New Delhi1,484National Capital Territory with its own Legislative Assembly (Article 239AA, 69th Amendment 1991).
5Jammu & KashmirSrinagar (summer) / Jammu (winter)42,241Became a UT with legislature on 31 October 2019, after the J&K Reorganisation Act, 2019.
6LadakhLeh59,146Carved out as a UT without legislature on 31 October 2019.
7LakshadweepKavaratti32Smallest UT by area; coral-island territory in the Arabian Sea.
8PuducherryPuducherry490Former French colonial territory; has its own Legislative Assembly.

India's District Map

India currently has approximately 780+ districts across all states and UTs combined — and this number keeps changing as state governments periodically create new districts by splitting existing ones. The figures below are widely-cited approximate counts; always verify the exact current count from each state's official gazette before quoting it in an exam.

State / UTApprox. DistrictsState / UTApprox. Districts
Uttar Pradesh75Madhya Pradesh55
Rajasthan~50Bihar38
Tamil Nadu38Maharashtra36
Assam35Karnataka31
Gujarat33Chhattisgarh33
Telangana33Odisha30
West Bengal~23–30Andhra Pradesh26
Arunachal Pradesh~26Jharkhand24
Punjab23Haryana22
Jammu & Kashmir (UT)20Manipur16
Nagaland16Kerala14
Uttarakhand13Himachal Pradesh12
Meghalaya12Mizoram11
Delhi (UT)11Tripura8
Sikkim6Puducherry (UT)4
A&N Islands (UT)3DNH & Daman-Diu (UT)3
Goa2Ladakh (UT)2
Chandigarh (UT)1Lakshadweep (UT)1

⚠️ District counts change frequently (e.g., Rajasthan, West Bengal, Andhra Pradesh have all revised counts in recent years through creation/merger of districts). Treat these as approximate, exam-prep reference figures only.

Records & Superlatives

A favourite Prelims question type — "largest/smallest" facts about Indian states, UTs, and districts.

📏 Largest State (Area)
Rajasthan — India's largest state by geographical area.
📏 Smallest State (Area)
Goa — India's smallest state by geographical area.
👥 Largest State (Population)
Uttar Pradesh — India's most populous state.
👥 Smallest State (Population)
Sikkim — India's least populous state.
🗺️ Largest UT (Area)
Ladakh — by far the largest Union Territory by area.
🗺️ Smallest UT (Area)
Lakshadweep — India's smallest Union Territory by area.
🏙️ Most Districts
Uttar Pradesh has the highest number of districts (75) among all states.
🏙️ Fewest Districts
Among full states, Goa has the fewest districts (2).
🌴 Longest Coastline (State)
Gujarat has the longest coastline among Indian states.
🏔️ Highest State Capital
Gangtok (Sikkim) and Shimla (Himachal Pradesh) are among India's highest-altitude state capitals.
🆕 Newest State
Telangana — formed 2 June 2014 by bifurcating Andhra Pradesh, India's 29th state.
🆕 Newest UTs
Jammu & Kashmir and Ladakh — created 31 October 2019, the most recent Union Territories.
🌐 Most International Borders Touched
Jammu & Kashmir/Ladakh and the North-Eastern states collectively share borders with the most neighbouring countries.
🏝️ Only Coral-Island UT
Lakshadweep — India's only Union Territory made up entirely of coral atolls.

How India's Map Was Redrawn

India's internal boundaries were not fixed at Independence — they have evolved through linguistic reorganisation, bifurcations, and special constitutional moves, several of which remain high-yield Polity & Modern History topics.

1950 — Part A/B/C/D States
At the Constitution's commencement, states were classified into Part A (former Governor's provinces), Part B (former princely states/unions), Part C (centrally administered), and Part D (Andaman & Nicobar).
1953 — Andhra State Created
India's first state formed purely on linguistic lines, carved out of Madras State — a precursor to the broader reorganisation that followed.
1956 — States Reorganisation Act
Based on the Fazl Ali Commission's report, states were reorganised primarily along linguistic lines — the single biggest redraw of India's internal map.
1960 — Bombay State Split
Bombay State was bifurcated into Gujarat (Gujarati-speaking) and Maharashtra (Marathi-speaking).
1963 — Nagaland Formed
Carved out of Assam, becoming the first state created out of the North-East Frontier region after 1956.
1966 — Punjab Reorganisation Act
Punjab was split along linguistic lines into Punjab (Punjabi-speaking) and Haryana (Hindi-speaking), with Himachal Pradesh's hill areas transferred and Chandigarh made a joint-capital UT.
1972 — North-East Reorganisation
Meghalaya, Manipur, and Tripura attained full statehood on the same day, 21 January 1972; Arunachal Pradesh and Mizoram remained Union Territories at the time.
1975 — Sikkim's Merger
Following a referendum, Sikkim merged with India as its 22nd state via the 36th Constitutional Amendment, ending its status as a protectorate.
1987 — Goa, Mizoram, Arunachal Pradesh
All three attained full statehood in 1987; Goa was split from the erstwhile Goa, Daman and Diu UT, with Daman & Diu remaining a separate UT.
2000 — Three New States
Chhattisgarh (from Madhya Pradesh), Uttarakhand (from Uttar Pradesh), and Jharkhand (from Bihar) were all created in the same year.
2014 — Telangana Carved Out
Andhra Pradesh was bifurcated to create Telangana — India's 29th and most recent full state, with Hyderabad as a joint capital for a transitional period.
2019 — J&K Reorganisation
The Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act, 2019 split the erstwhile state into two Union Territories — Jammu & Kashmir (with legislature) and Ladakh (without legislature) — alongside the reading down of Article 370's special provisions.
2020 — DNH & Daman-Diu Merged
The two separate Union Territories — Dadra & Nagar Haveli, and Daman & Diu — were merged into a single UT effective 26 January 2020.

Constitutional Special Status & Schedules

Several states and regions enjoy special constitutional provisions — a recurring Polity Mains topic, especially around Articles 370, 371, and the Fifth & Sixth Schedules.

Article 370 (Historical)
Granted special autonomous status to the former state of Jammu & Kashmir; its operative provisions were read down by a Presidential Order in August 2019, alongside the state's reorganisation into two UTs.
Article 371 — Maharashtra & Gujarat
Special provisions for the development of Vidarbha, Marathwada, Saurashtra, and Kutch regions, including separate development boards.
Article 371A — Nagaland
Protects Naga customary law, religious/social practices, and ownership of land & resources; Parliament cannot legislate on these without the State Assembly's consent.
Article 371B/C/G — NE States
Provide special arrangements for Assam (371B), Manipur (371C), and Mizoram (371G) tribal/hill areas, including special committees for hill regions.
Article 371D/E — Andhra Pradesh
Provides for equitable opportunities in public employment & education across regions; 371E enabled the establishment of a Central University in Andhra Pradesh.
Article 371F — Sikkim
Special provisions protecting Sikkim's existing laws and seats reserved in its Legislative Assembly, following its 1975 merger with India.
Article 371H/I — Arunachal Pradesh & Goa
371H gives the Arunachal Pradesh Governor special responsibility for law and order; 371I sets a minimum strength for the Goa Legislative Assembly.
Fifth Schedule
Governs the administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in states other than Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram — empowering Tribal Advisory Councils.
Sixth Schedule
Provides for Autonomous District & Regional Councils with legislative, executive, and limited judicial powers in tribal areas of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura, and Mizoram.
Article 239AA — Delhi
Inserted by the 69th Amendment (1991), gives Delhi (NCT) a Legislative Assembly and Council of Ministers, with land, police, and public order remaining with the Union.

Note: District counts, capitals, and special-provision details can change through state legislation or Presidential Orders — always cross-check the latest figures against the relevant State Gazette, Census of India, and Ministry of Home Affairs notifications before quoting them in exams.

BharatCivilServices
Union Public Service Commission

UPSC Civil Services

The premier examination system for Group A & B Central Government Services — IAS, IPS, IFS, IRS, IRTS, and 24+ allied services.

Static data — tap Sync with AI
UPSC

Civil Services Exam (CSE)

IAS, IPS, IRS, IRTS, IFS & 24 Allied Services

Age21 – 32 yrs (relaxation as applicable)
QualificationAny Graduation (final year eligible)
Attempts6 (General); 9 (OBC); Unlimited (SC/ST)
FrequencyOnce a year (Feb notification)
Vacancies~900 per year
3-Stage Selection
  • Prelims — GS Paper I (200 marks) + CSAT Paper II (qualifying 33%)
  • Mains — 9 papers: Essay + 4 GS + 2 Optional + 2 language (qualifying)
  • Personality Test (Interview) — 275 marks
  • Final merit = Mains (1750) + Interview (275) = 2025 marks
Preparation Strategy
  • NCERT 6–12 first, then standard references (Laxmikant, Spectrum, etc.)
  • Daily newspaper reading — The Hindu / Indian Express
  • Optional subject selection based on background + scoring potential
  • Answer writing practice from Day 1 — Mains is the real battle
Official UPSC Site
IAS

IAS — Indian Administrative Service

Highest civil service — District Collector, Secretary, Cabinet positions

CadreState Cadres (28 cadres)
Rank RequiredTop ~100 ranks typically
TrainingLBSNAA, Mussoorie — 2 years
Pay Scale₹56,100 – ₹2,25,000 (Level 10+)
Career Trajectory
  • SDM → DM/Collector → Divisional Commissioner
  • Secretary to State Government
  • Joint Secretary / Additional Secretary (GoI)
  • Secretary to Government of India (Cabinet)
Key Focus Areas
  • Polity, Governance, Public Administration
  • Ethics & Integrity (GS Paper IV)
  • State-specific knowledge for cadre preference
  • Leadership qualities for Interview
IPS

IPS — Indian Police Service

Law enforcement leadership — SP, DIG, IG, DGP

CadreState Police Cadres
Rank Required~100–200 ranks typically
TrainingSVPNPA Hyderabad — 2 years
Physical StandardHeight & chest standards apply
Career Trajectory
  • ASP → SP → SSP → DIG → IG
  • ADG → DGP (State Police Chief)
  • Central deputation: CBI, IB, CRPF, BSF, NSG
  • DG-rank postings at Central organisations
Key Focus Areas
  • Internal Security (GS Paper III)
  • Disaster Management, Cyber Security
  • Criminal law basics for Interview
  • Physical fitness to meet medical standards
IFS

IFS — Indian Foreign Service

Diplomatic corps — Ambassadors, High Commissioners

Rank RequiredTop ~30 ranks
TrainingFSOI, New Delhi + Foreign Postings
LanguagesCompulsory foreign language training
Pay Scale₹56,100 + Foreign Allowances
Career Trajectory
  • Third Secretary → Second Secretary → First Secretary
  • Counsellor → Deputy Chief of Mission
  • Ambassador / High Commissioner
  • Secretary (East/West) → Foreign Secretary
Key Focus Areas
  • International Relations deeply (GS II)
  • India's foreign policy history
  • Optional: Political Science & IR or History
  • Strong communication for Interview
IRS

IRS — Indian Revenue Service

Income Tax & Customs & GST — Revenue administration

CadresIRS (IT) & IRS (C&CE)
Rank RequiredTypically 200–500 range
TrainingNADT Nagpur / NACIN
MinistryFinance / Revenue
Career Trajectory
  • ITO → ACIT → DCIT → JCIT → Addl. CIT
  • CIT → PCIT → CCIT → Member CBDT
  • Chairman CBDT (apex)
Key Focus Areas
  • Economy & Budget (GS III)
  • Taxation law & GST basics
  • Finance optional can help score
ALLIED

Allied Civil Services

IRTS, IRAS, IAAS, IDAS, IOFS & more via CSE

IRTSIndian Railway Traffic Service
IRASIndian Railway Accounts Service
IAASIndian Audit & Accounts Service
IDASIndian Defence Accounts Service
IOFSIndian Ordnance Factories Service
Selection
  • Same CSE exam — preference order in DAF
  • Lower ranks allocated to allied services
  • All have excellent career growth & perks
Strategy
  • Fill DAF preferences wisely
  • Research each service's work profile before preference
  • Focus on clearing Mains well — rank determines service
UPSC

Indian Forest Service (IFS)

Forest Officer — Wildlife, Ecology & Environment

Age21 – 32 years
QualificationGraduation in Science / Engineering / Agri / Vet / Medicine
FrequencyOnce a year (combined with CSE Prelims)
Vacancies~100 per year
3-Stage Selection
  • Prelims — Shared with UPSC CSE Prelims
  • IFoS Mains — 6 papers: GS + 2 optional forest subjects
  • Personality Test — 300 marks
Preparation Strategy
  • Botany, Zoology, Forestry, Agriculture common optionals
  • Ecology, Biodiversity, Forest Laws critical
  • Physical standards: medical fitness mandatory
  • Prelims prep shared with CSE — efficient dual approach
Official UPSC Site
UPSC

Engineering Services Exam (IES/ESE)

Class-I technical services — Railway, PWD, BRO, DRDO, CPWD, Telecom

Age21 – 30 years
QualificationB.E./B.Tech in relevant engineering
StreamsCivil, Mechanical, Electrical, Electronics
FrequencyOnce a year
3-Stage Selection
  • Prelims — Paper I (GS + Engineering Aptitude) + Paper II (Technical)
  • Mains — 2 Technical Papers (Conventional)
  • Personality Test — 200 marks
Preparation Strategy
  • Deep technical subject mastery in your stream
  • GS component: basic science, environment, current affairs
  • Solve 10+ years ESE previous papers (subject-wise)
  • ACE Academy, Made Easy materials widely used
Official UPSC Site
UPSC

CAPF (AC) — Central Armed Police Forces

Assistant Commandant — CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB

Age20 – 25 years
QualificationAny Graduation
ForcesCRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB
FrequencyOnce a year
Selection Process
  • Written Exam — Paper I: GS & Essay (250 marks), Paper II: GS (250 marks)
  • Physical Efficiency Test (PET)
  • Medical Examination
  • Personal Interview — 150 marks
Preparation Strategy
  • Internal Security, Defence, Border issues (GS focus)
  • Essay writing practice critical
  • Physical fitness: running, chin-ups standards
  • Current affairs: border disputes, terrorism, Naxalism

  • Mains — GS + Odia Language + Optional

BharatCivilServices
Govt. Officer Classification

Group A & B Posts — Explained

This platform covers only Group A & B civil and administrative services — gazetted officer and supervisory ranks under UPSC, SSC CGL (Group A & B posts only), and State PCS. Group C and D (clerical & support staff) are out of scope.

Static data — tap Sync with AI
GROUP A

Group A — Gazetted, Senior

Top-tier officer ranks, policy-making & administrative authority

CentralIAS, IPS, IFS, IRS, IES, IFoS, IRTS, CMS, IES (Economic)
StateState PCS (Top posts: SDM, DSP, BDO, Tehsildar)
EntryUPSC CSE, RPSC/UPPSC/MPPSC etc. Mains+Interview
GROUP B

Group B — Gazetted/Non-Gazetted

Mid-level officer & supervisory posts

CentralSSC CGL (Section Officer, Inspector), CAPF Asst. Commandant
StateNaib Tehsildar, BDO, Block-level officers via State PCS
EntrySSC CGL Tier I–IV, State PCS lower posts
Complete Preparation Guide

Preparation Hub — Group A & B

Syllabus, exam pattern, recommended books, phase-wise study plan & strategy — for Group A (UPSC CSE / IFoS / IES / PCS Top) and Group B (SSC CGL ASO/Inspector/AAO & PCS Lower). Each Group needs a different approach.

SYLLABUS

Group A — Full Syllabus Breakdown

UPSC CSE / State PCS (top posts) — three-stage exam

Prelims (GS Paper I — 200 marks)
  • History of India & Indian National Movement
  • Indian & World Geography (Physical, Social, Economic)
  • Indian Polity & Governance — Constitution, Political System, Panchayati Raj
  • Economic & Social Development — Sustainable Dev., Poverty, Inclusion
  • Environmental Ecology, Biodiversity & Climate Change
  • General Science
  • Current National & International events
Prelims (CSAT Paper II — 200 marks, qualifying 33%)
  • Comprehension & English Language
  • Logical Reasoning & Analytical Ability
  • Basic Numeracy (Class X level) & Data Interpretation
Mains (9 papers, ~1750 marks)
  • Essay (250 marks)
  • GS I — Indian Heritage, Culture, History, Geography of World & Society
  • GS II — Governance, Constitution, Polity, Social Justice, International Relations
  • GS III — Technology, Economic Development, Biodiversity, Security, Disaster Mgmt
  • GS IV — Ethics, Integrity & Aptitude
  • Optional Subject — Paper I & II (500 marks)
  • Qualifying: Indian Language paper + English paper (300 marks each)
Interview (275 marks)
  • Personality test based on DAF (Detailed Application Form)
  • Current affairs, ethical dilemmas, home-state awareness
BOOKS

Recommended Books & Resources

Subject-wise standard reference list

NCERTsClass 6–12 History, Geography, Polity, Economics, Science
HistorySpectrum (Modern), Bipan Chandra (Freedom Struggle)
PolityM. Laxmikant — Indian Polity
GeographyG.C. Leong, NCERT + Atlas (Orient Blackswan)
EconomyRamesh Singh, Economic Survey, Union Budget
EnvironmentShankar IAS Environment
EthicsLexicon for Ethics, 2nd ARC Report
Current AffairsThe Hindu / Indian Express + monthly compilations (Vision IAS, Insights)
PYQUPSC Prelims & Mains last 15 years — non-negotiable
Month 1–6 — Foundation
  • NCERT line-by-line + basic standard books
  • Start daily newspaper reading habit
  • Make short notes, not photocopies of books
Month 7–10 — Advanced + Optional
  • Standard books for each GS paper
  • Pick & start Optional subject seriously
  • Begin daily answer writing (Mains pattern)
Month 11–13 — Mains Focus
  • Complete Optional syllabus + PYQ analysis
  • Join a Mains test series
  • Daily current affairs note-making (static + dynamic linking)
Month 14–15 — Prelims Sprint
  • Full-length Prelims mocks — 5/week
  • Revise only short notes & mind maps
  • CSAT practice — qualifying marks only needed
Post-Mains — Interview
  • DAF-based mock interviews (5–10 boards)
  • Revise home-state & optional subject thoroughly
  • Stay updated on last 6 months' current affairs
SYLLABUS

Group B — Full Syllabus Breakdown

SSC CGL / CAPF AC / State PCS lower posts — multi-tier exam

Tier I — 200 marks, 60 min, CBT
  • General Intelligence & Reasoning (25Q)
  • General Awareness — Static GK + Current Affairs (25Q)
  • Quantitative Aptitude (25Q)
  • English Comprehension (25Q)
Tier II — Multiple sessions
  • Quantitative Abilities
  • English Language & Comprehension
  • Statistics (for JSO posts) / Finance & Economics (for AAO posts)
  • General Studies — Finance & Economics (select posts)
Skill/Document/Physical (post-specific)
  • Data Entry Speed Test (for Tax Asst.)
  • Document Verification + Medical (for SI posts)
BOOKS

Recommended Books & Resources

Subject-wise reference list for SSC-level exams

QuantR.S. Aggarwal — Quantitative Aptitude, Rakesh Yadav Class Notes
ReasoningR.S. Aggarwal — Reasoning, M.K. Pandey Analytical Reasoning
EnglishWren & Martin, SSC English by Neetu Singh / Plinth to Paramount
GK/GSLucent's GK, current affairs monthly digest
PracticeSSC CGL Tier I & II PYQ — last 10 years, daily mocks
Month 1–2 — Basics
  • Clear concepts in Quant (arithmetic + advanced maths)
  • Reasoning fundamentals — verbal & non-verbal
  • Build vocabulary + grammar rules for English
Month 3–4 — Speed Building
  • Topic-wise timed practice sets
  • Daily current affairs (15–20 min)
  • Sectional mocks for each Tier I subject
Month 5–6 — Full Mocks
  • Full-length Tier I mocks — analyse errors daily
  • Start Tier II prep (Statistics/Finance if applicable)
  • Revise GK via short notes
BharatCivilServices
Know Your Goal

Career Pathways

Choose your educational background or career goal and find the right civil services exam path for you.

📚
Arts / Humanities Graduate
History, Political Science, Geography, Sociology, Literature backgrounds — ideal for CSE with scoring optionals.
UPSC CSE · State PCS
🔬
Science Graduate
Physics, Chemistry, Botany, Zoology backgrounds — best fit for IFoS, ESE Science, or CSE with science optional.
IFoS · ESE · CSE
⚙️
Engineering Graduate
B.Tech/B.E. graduates excel in IES/ESE, CSE (with Maths or Engineering optional), and CAPF.
IES/ESE · CSE · CAPF
⚖️
Law Graduate (LLB)
LLB holders have a strong edge in UPSC CSE with Law as a scoring optional, plus UPSC EPFO/AO legal officer posts.
UPSC CSE · Law Optional
📊
Commerce / Finance Graduate
B.Com, CA, MBA Finance graduates can target IRS (Income Tax / C&CE), IBPS Officer cadre, SSC CGL (Group B), and UPSC CSE.
IRS · IBPS · SSC CGL (Gr.B)

Arts / Humanities Graduate Path

Arts and Humanities graduates are arguably the most suited for the UPSC Civil Services Examination. Optionals like History, Political Science & IR, Sociology, Public Administration, Geography, and Philosophy have historically yielded top rankers.

Recommended Exam Path

  • UPSC CSE — Choose optional from History / Pol.Sci&IR / Geography / Sociology / Philosophy
  • State PCS — UPPCS, BPSC, MPPSC, RPSC etc. with GS focus
  • SSC CGL (Group A & B posts only) — ASO/Inspector/AAO without optional burden

Scoring Optionals for Arts Students

  • Geography — consistent scoring, high-value diagrams
  • Sociology — conceptual, scoring in Mains
  • History — if strong in Indian History
  • Public Administration — popular, NCERT-aligned

Science Graduate Path

Science graduates have excellent opportunities across multiple competitive exams. The IFoS (Indian Forest Service) specifically requires a science background, and optionals like Botany, Zoology, Chemistry, Physics, and Mathematics perform well in UPSC Mains.

Recommended Exam Path

  • Indian Forest Service (IFoS) — Science degree mandatory, Prelims shared with CSE
  • UPSC CSE — Science optionals: Physics / Chemistry / Zoology / Botany / Maths
  • SSC JE / IES (GS) — For basic science graduates

Key Advantage

  • Environment & Ecology section in GS III — natural strength area
  • Science papers can be highly scoring with formula-based answers

Engineering Graduate Path

Engineering graduates have one of the widest arrays of civil services options. IES/ESE is specifically designed for engineers, and CSE with Maths or Engineering optional is very competitive.

Recommended Exam Path

  • UPSC IES/ESE — Civil, Mechanical, Electrical, Electronics streams
  • UPSC CSE — Optionals: Maths, Mechanical Engg, Electrical Engg, Civil Engg
  • CAPF (AC) — Good option for balanced preparation
  • SSC JE (Group B Gazetted) — Junior Engineer in CPWD, CWC, MES — technical officer cadre

Law Graduate (LLB) Path

LLB graduates have a strong advantage in UPSC CSE where Law is a scoring optional, and several UPSC legal-officer posts are open through dedicated notifications.

Recommended Exam Path

  • UPSC CSE — Law as optional: structured, scoring, and GS-aligned
  • State PCS — Law background helps in GS essay and ethics papers
  • UPSC EPFO / AO Posts — Legal Officer posts via UPSC

Law Optional Key Topics for UPSC CSE

  • Constitutional Law — Articles, amendments, landmark judgments
  • International Law — Treaties, ICJ, UN conventions
  • Administrative Law — Judicial review, natural justice
  • Law of Crimes & Torts — IPC/BNS fundamentals

Commerce / Finance Graduate Path

Commerce and finance graduates are well-suited for IRS (Income Tax / C&CE), banking services, and SSC CGL posts like Income Tax Inspector and Auditor.

Recommended Exam Path

  • UPSC CSE — Optional: Commerce & Accountancy (scoring for B.Com)
  • IRS via CSE — Revenue services at mid-rank range
  • IBPS PO / SO (Finance) — Banking officer cadre (Group A/B equivalent)
  • SSC CGL (Group A & B) — Auditor, Accountant, Income Tax Inspector (Group B Gazetted)
BharatCivilServices
Topper's Blueprint

Preparation Strategy

Phase-wise roadmap & strategy guide for Group A (UPSC CSE / IFoS / IES / State PCS top posts) and Group B (SSC CGL ASO/Inspector/AAO, CAPF-AC, State PCS lower posts). Each Group needs a different approach.

Static data — tap Sync with AI

GROUP A — IAS / IPS / IFS / State PCS (Top Posts)

UPSC CSE is the toughest exam in India. Average selection timeline: 12–18 months of dedicated preparation. Strategy is built on depth over breadth — a Prelims-Mains-Interview three-stage ladder.

Phase 1 — Foundation (3–6 Months)
  • NCERT 6–12: History, Geography, Polity, Science, Economics
  • Spectrum Modern History
  • Laxmikant — Indian Polity (cover to cover)
  • Ramesh Singh — Economy
  • Certificate Physical Geography (G.C. Leong)
  • Daily newspaper: The Hindu / Indian Express (1 hr/day)
Phase 2 — Advanced GS (3–4 Months)
  • Environment: Shankar IAS book
  • Science & Tech: Ravi P. Agrahari
  • Ethics: Lexicon or ARC 4th Report
  • IR: Pavneet Singh, MEA website
  • Economy: Budget + Economic Survey (annual)
  • Mains GS PYQ: solve & analyse last 10 years
Phase 3 — Optional Subject (4–6 Months)
  • Choose optional by May of Year 1
  • Gather subject-specific sources (2–3 key books)
  • PYQ analysis: Optional is your scoring weapon
  • Join optional test series if possible
  • Target 270+ in optional for competitive score
Phase 4 — Answer Writing (Ongoing)
  • Start answer writing from Month 3 — don't delay
  • UPSC Mains: 200-word / 150-word format
  • Introduction + 3–4 structured points + Conclusion
  • Use data, diagrams, examples wherever possible
  • Join a test series — feedback loop is critical
Phase 5 — Prelims Gear-Up (2–3 Months)
  • Solve UPSC PYQ Prelims: 2011–2024
  • 5 full mock tests per week in the final month
  • Current Affairs: Vision IAS / Insights monthly
  • CSAT: Maths + Comprehension to qualify 33%
  • Revise: short notes / mind maps only
Phase 6 — Interview Prep
  • DAF (Detailed Application Form) is the Interview
  • Know everything in your DAF deeply
  • State-specific knowledge for your home state
  • Current affairs: last 6 months before interview
  • Mock interviews: 5–10 before the actual board

GROUP B — SSC CGL (ASO / Inspector / AAO) · CAPF AC · State PCS Lower Posts

Group B exams target gazetted & supervisory officer posts — only SSC CGL Group A & B posts (ASO in CSS/Railway Board, Inspector CBI/Income Tax, AAO C&AG) are covered. Objective-heavy, no optional subject, clearable in 6–10 months. SSC CGL: Tier I & II CBT, descriptive (where applicable), document verification.

Phase 1 — Core Subjects (2–3 Months)
  • Quantitative Aptitude: R.S. Aggarwal / Rakesh Yadav
  • English: Plinth to Paramount / Mirror of Common Errors
  • Reasoning: R.S. Aggarwal Verbal & Non-Verbal
  • General Awareness: Lucent GK + Monthly Current Affairs
  • Daily: 50 questions each subject — speed matters
Phase 2 — Advanced Maths & English (2 Months)
  • Maths: Algebra, Geometry, Trigonometry, Mensuration (CGL Tier II)
  • English: Cloze test, Reading Comprehension, Error Spotting
  • Statistics (for JSO post): if targeting Statistical Investigator
  • Finance & Economics (for AAO): if targeting Assistant Audit Officer
Phase 3 — Mock Tests & Speed Drills (2 Months)
  • Full mocks: Tier I format — 100 questions in 60 minutes
  • Target 140+/200 in Tier I for safe cutoff
  • Analyse every wrong answer — error log mandatory
  • Tier II mock series: 60-min Maths + 60-min English daily
Phase 4 — Tier III Descriptive (1 Month)
  • Essay writing: 200–250 words on current topics
  • Letter/Application writing: formal format practice
  • Précis writing: summarise a passage in 1/3rd words
  • Time management: 60 minutes for full paper
CAPF AC — Additional Focus
  • CAPF Paper I: GS + Maths (same as SSC CGL Tier I)
  • CAPF Paper II: Essay + Comprehension + Précis (2.5 hrs)
  • Physical Test: 100m, 800m/1.6km run, long jump, shot put
  • Medical standard: height, chest, eyesight requirements
  • Interview / Personality Test: UPSC board conducts
Key Tips for Group B
  • Speed + Accuracy > depth — 60 min for 100 Qs, no negative marking in Tier II
  • Attempt SSC CGL (Group A & B) along with State PCS — common GS overlap ~60%
  • State PCS lower posts: same GS prep, add state-specific GK
  • Sectional cutoffs exist in Tier I — don't ignore any subject
BharatCivilServices

Pomodoro-style focus timer — proven to boost UPSC preparation discipline.

25:00
FOCUS SESSION

BharatCivilServices
Life After Selection

Career Explorer

Pay scale, promotion ladder, posting authority, and real-world power — for every Group A, B, C & D government post.

Static data — tap Sync with AI
Group A — Senior Gazetted Officers (IAS/IPS/IFS/IES/CAPF)  ·  Entry via UPSC CSE / IFoS / IES / CAPF  ·  Pay Level 10–18 (₹56,100–₹2,25,000)  ·  All India / Central Service cadres
🏛️
IAS — Indian Administrative Service
₹56,100 → ₹2,25,000
The apex of civil services. SDM → District Collector / DM → Divisional Commissioner → Secretary to State Govt → Joint Secretary GoI → Additional Secretary → Secretary → Cabinet Secretary. Controls all district-level administration, law & order coordination, development programmes.
Entry: UPSC CSE  |  Cadre: State (All India)  |  Deputation: Central Secretariat, PSUs, UN bodies
🚔
IPS — Indian Police Service
₹56,100 → ₹2,25,000
ASP → SP → SSP → DIG → IG → ADG → DGP. State cadre posting: District SP, Range DIG, Zonal IG. Central deputation: CBI Director, IB, NSG, CRPF DG, BSF DG, NIA, RAW. Handles law enforcement, crime investigation, internal security.
Entry: UPSC CSE  |  Cadre: State (All India)  |  Deputation: CBI, IB, NSG, CRPF, RAW
🌿
IFS — Indian Forest Service
₹56,100 → ₹2,25,000
Range Forest Officer → DFO (Divisional Forest Officer) → Conservator → CCF → PCCF (Principal Chief Conservator). Manages national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, forest land, carbon credits, green India mission. Also posted to MoEFCC, NTCA, WII.
Entry: UPSC IFoS (separate exam)  |  Cadre: State (All India)  |  Deputation: MoEFCC, NTCA, FAO
✈️
IFS — Indian Foreign Service
₹56,100 + Foreign Allowances
Third Secretary → Second Secretary → First Secretary → Counsellor → Minister → Ambassador / High Commissioner. Posted at Indian Embassies/Consulates worldwide. MEA career: Under Secretary → Director → Joint Secretary → Additional Secretary → Foreign Secretary.
Entry: UPSC CSE  |  Cadre: Central (MEA)  |  Perks: Foreign posting, diplomatic immunity
💰
IRS — Indian Revenue Service
₹56,100 → ₹1,44,200
IRS (IT): Income Tax Officer → DCIT → ACIT → CIT → PCCIT → Chairman CBDT. IRS (C&CE): AC/DC → JC → Commissioner → DG → Chairman CBIC. Handles tax administration, customs, GST, enforcement, PMLA investigations, international taxation.
Entry: UPSC CSE  |  Cadre: Central (CBDT/CBIC)  |  Deputation: ED, CBI, SFIO
⚙️
IES — Indian Engineering Service
₹56,100 → ₹1,44,200
Assistant Executive Engineer → Executive Engineer → Superintending Engineer → Chief Engineer → DG (Works). Streams: Civil, Mechanical, Electrical, Electronics. Departments: CPWD, Railways, MES, BRO, Doordarshan, Posts, Defence R&D.
Entry: UPSC IES/ESE  |  Qualification: B.Tech/B.E.  |  Departments: CPWD, Railways, BRO, MES
🛡️
CAPF — Assistant Commandant
₹56,100 → ₹1,12,400
Assistant Commandant → Deputy Commandant → Commandant → DIG → IG → ADG → DG. Forces: CRPF, BSF, CISF, ITBP, SSB, NSG, AR. Handles border security, internal security, counter-insurgency, VIP protection, industrial security.
Entry: UPSC CAPF (AC)  |  Physical: Mandatory fitness test  |  Uniform: Yes
🗺️
State PCS Officer (Top Posts)
₹56,100 → ₹1,44,200
SDM (Sub-Divisional Magistrate) → Additional Collector → District Magistrate / Collector → Divisional Commissioner → Principal Secretary. DSP → SP (State Cadre). BDO → CDO. Strong field authority — controls land records, development, law & order at block/district level.
Entry: UPPSC / BPSC / MPPSC / RPSC etc.  |  Cadre: State  |  Promotion: Via DPC / State Govt
Group B — Gazetted & Non-Gazetted Officers  ·  Entry via SSC CGL / CAPF / State PCS lower  ·  Pay Level 7–10 (₹44,900–₹1,12,400)  ·  Supervisory & mid-level posts
📊
SSC CGL — Income Tax Inspector
₹44,900 → ₹1,12,400
Income Tax Inspector (ITI) → Tax Assistant → Sr. Tax Assistant → ITO (Income Tax Officer via departmental exam). Under CBDT. Conducts raids, assessments, refund processing, TDS verification. One of the most coveted SSC CGL posts. Group B Gazetted after promotion to ITO.
Entry: SSC CGL  |  Dept: CBDT (Income Tax)  |  Level: 7 (₹44,900)
🚨
SSC CGL — Sub-Inspector (CBI/NIA)
₹35,400 → ₹1,12,400
Sub-Inspector → Inspector → DSP (via LDCE). CBI SI handles high-profile corruption, fraud, economic offences. NIA SI handles terror cases. One of the most powerful field posts at Group B level. Excellent growth via departmental promotion to IPS deputation posts.
Entry: SSC CGL  |  Dept: CBI / NIA  |  Level: 6 (₹35,400)
🏢
SSC CGL — Section Officer
₹44,900 → ₹1,42,400
Assistant Section Officer (ASO) → Section Officer → Under Secretary → Deputy Secretary → Director → Joint Secretary. Central Secretariat Services (CSS) — policy drafting, file movement, coordination between ministries. Highly prestigious desk officer career path.
Entry: SSC CGL  |  Dept: Central Secretariat (CSS)  |  Level: 7
🛃
SSC CGL — Inspector (Customs/GST)
₹44,900 → ₹1,12,400
Inspector → Superintendent → Assistant Commissioner → Deputy Commissioner → Commissioner. Under CBIC. Handles goods clearance, anti-smuggling, GST audit, valuation. Posted at ports, airports, land customs stations, GST commissionerates across India.
Entry: SSC CGL  |  Dept: CBIC (Customs & GST)  |  Level: 7
📡
SSC CGL — Statistical Investigator
₹44,900 → ₹1,12,400
Statistical Investigator Gr. II → Gr. I → Senior Statistical Officer → Deputy Director (Statistics) → Director. Under NSO (National Statistical Office), M/o Statistics. Data collection, Census, PLFS, NSSO surveys. Requires Maths/Statistics background — scoring optional in CGL Tier II.
Entry: SSC CGL (JSO post)  |  Dept: NSO / MoSPI  |  Qualification: Maths/Statistics graduate
🗺️
State PCS — Naib Tehsildar / BDO
₹29,200 → ₹92,300
Naib Tehsildar → Tehsildar → Additional Collector (Revenue). BDO (Block Development Officer) → ADCO → CDO. Controls land revenue, mutation, property disputes at block/tehsil level. Highly powerful local-level post — direct contact with public and elected representatives.
Entry: State PCS lower (UPPCS/BPSC etc.)  |  Cadre: State  |  Level: Group B Non-Gazetted
🔬
SSC JE — Junior Engineer
₹35,400 → ₹1,12,400
Junior Engineer → Assistant Engineer → Executive Engineer → Superintending Engineer. Departments: CPWD, MES, BRO, CWC, FARAKKA Barrage, NMCG, Doordarshan. Civil / Mechanical / Electrical streams. Site inspection, project execution, quality control for govt. infrastructure.
Entry: SSC JE  |  Qualification: Diploma / B.Tech  |  Dept: CPWD, MES, BRO
BharatCivilServices
Stay Updated

Current Affairs Hub

Daily and monthly current affairs are the backbone of Prelims, Mains, and Interview — for UPSC as well as every State PCS exam.

Static sources — tap Sync with AI
DAILY

Daily News Analysis

Editorials & national dailies analysed daily for exam relevance

Recommended Sources
  • The Hindu — Editorial & Op-Ed pages
  • Indian Express — Explained section
  • PIB (Press Information Bureau) — official releases
  • PRS Legislative Research — Bill/Act summaries
Open PIB
MONTHLY

Monthly Compilation

Consolidated revision before the next month begins

Focus Areas
  • Government schemes & policy launches
  • Reports & Indices (NITI Aayog, World Bank, UN)
  • Appointments, Awards, Summits, Defence deals
  • Science & Tech breakthroughs, ISRO/DRDO updates
YEARLY

Yearbook & Budget

Static + dynamic linkage for Mains answer writing

Must-Cover
  • Union Budget — key allocations & schemes
  • Economic Survey of India highlights
  • India Year Book (Publications Division)
  • State of the Economy / RBI Annual Report
Union Budget
MAGAZINE

Govt. Monthly Magazines

Free official publications for static + current linkage

Free Picks
  • Kurukshetra — rural development (monthly)
  • Yojana — policy & governance (monthly)
  • Employment News — exam notices & articles

Solving PYQs reveals the exact pattern, difficulty, and recurring themes of every exam — the single highest-ROI prep activity.

  • Mains GS I–IV + Essay Get Papers ↗

    10 carefully curated questions covering Polity, History, Geography, Economy, Environment & Current Affairs.

    Modern Indian History

    Master The Mains

    Answer Writing Practice

    Daily Mains-style questions with built-in timer, word counter, structure prompts, and a self-evaluation rubric used by toppers.

    Static questions — tap Sync with AI
    Today's Question
    Loading question…
    📌 GS Paper II 📝 150 words / 10 marks ⏱️ Recommended: 7–8 minutes
    Words: 0 Characters: 0 Reading time: 0s Target: 150 words Status: —
    📋 Self-Evaluation Rubric (After Writing)
    • Introduction (2): Did you define the topic & set context?
    • Directive followed (3): Did you answer "Discuss/Examine/Critically Analyse" properly?
    • Dimensions (3): Multiple angles — political, social, economic, ethical, etc.?
    • Examples & Data (2): Real-world cases, reports, court rulings, statistics?
    • Conclusion (2): Balanced, solution-oriented, futuristic?
    • Presentation (1): Used headings, flowchart, diagram, underlining where appropriate?
    • Word limit (1): Within ±10% of target?

    Tip: Score yourself out of 10 honestly. Trend matters more than score — track improvement over weeks.

    Today's Question (State PCS Mains)
    Loading question…
    📌 GS Paper II 📝 150 words / 10 marks ⏱️ Recommended: 7–8 minutes
    Words: 0 Characters: 0 Reading time: 0s Target: 150 words Status: —
    📋 Self-Evaluation Rubric (After Writing)
    • Introduction (2): Did you define the topic & set context?
    • Directive followed (3): Did you answer "Discuss/Examine/Critically Analyse" properly?
    • State-specific angle (3): Did you connect the answer to your target state's specific context, schemes, or geography where relevant?
    • Examples & Data (2): Real-world cases, reports, statistics?
    • Conclusion (2): Balanced, solution-oriented, futuristic?
    • Word limit (1): Within ±10% of target?

    Note: Exact word/marks scheme varies by state PSC — check your specific state's Mains pattern in the State PCS section and calibrate accordingly.

    📋

    SSC Exams Don't Have a Descriptive Mains

    SSC CGL, CHSL, MTS, and most subordinate-service exams are fully objective (MCQ-based) across all tiers — there's no essay or long-form answer to write or evaluate here.

    Instead, focus your prep time on the Mock Tests and Daily Quiz sections for speed and accuracy drills, which are what actually move your SSC score.

    BharatCivilServices
    Beat The Forgetting Curve

    Smart Revision Tracker

    Add any topic and our system schedules revisions based on the proven Ebbinghaus spaced repetition intervals — Day 1, 3, 7, 15, 30. Never forget what you study.

    ⚡ Due Today

    📚 All Tracked Topics

    No topics tracked yet. Add your first topic above to get started.

    🧠 The Science of Spaced Repetition

    Hermann Ebbinghaus's research shows we forget ~50% in 1 day, ~70% in a week without revision. Reviewing at increasing intervals (1d → 3d → 7d → 15d → 30d) transfers knowledge to long-term memory. Toppers swear by this method.
